text: My name is Dr. Ismanto Sukhandi. I'm a HPP surgeon at AdventHealth Tampa. It's an honor and privilege for me to present our video during SAGE's virtual meeting. This is about a robotic Roux-en-Y hepatic autogenostomy for a right hepatic duct transection during a laparoscopic olesostectomy.
The cholangiogram reveals multiple surgical clips placed at the entrance of the right hepatic duct, near the bifurcation of the biliary system.
The robotic surgical approach reveals an intact left hepatic system, while the right hepatic system appears non-existent or not visible. Five trocars are utilized for the operation.
The surgeon carefully dissects adhesions between the transverse colon and the gallbladder fossa, which are visible five days after the initial laparoscopic cholecystectomy.

The surgeon carefully dissects around the biloma collection in the subhepatic fossa, ensuring no injury occurs to the adjacent transverse colon. A clip is visible at the entrance of the right hepatic duct into the bifurcation.

The surgeon removes the PTC to facilitate liver mobilization cephalad, exposing the porta hepatis. The falciform ligament is dissected up to the hepatic oval confluence. A laparoscopic liver retractor elevates the liver, revealing the leaking right hepatic duct as the Haller plate is lowered to better visualize the anatomy.

The surgeon resects tissue with a Bovie electrocautery, revealing the lumen of the right hepatic duct. A catheter is inserted into the duct, and a cholangiogram is used to confirm the anatomical structure. The creation of the Roux limb is underway.

The surgeon identifies the ligament of Treitz and measures approximately thirty-five centimeters distally. A mesenteric window is created, followed by a stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy. The omentum is split down while ensuring the Roux limb remains vascularized.

The surgeon measures six centimeters of the Roux limb and creates a stapled side-to-side jejunojejunostomy using a robotic blue load stapler. An opening is made on both sides of the jejunum for anastomosis, and the common enterotomy is closed with a running suture using a 3-0 V-Loc suture.

The surgeon demonstrates robotic suturing techniques to fellows, starting with an anchoring stitch at the inferior corner of the common enterotomy. The edge of the bowel wall is imbricated to ensure a secure anastomosis. Another stitch is initiated from the opposite corner using the robotic system.

The surgeon ensures the sutures on the bowel wall are adequately tightened as they meet in the middle, completing the anastomosis. The bedside assistant assists in transposing the Roux limb. A small enterotomy is created in preparation for the hepatogastrojejunostomy.

The surgeon begins the anastomosis at the nine o'clock position, placing the index stitch on the right hepatic duct. The surrounding Glisson's capsule remains intact, indicating a high biliary anastomosis near the hilar plate, distal to the bifurcation. The robotic system aids in performing this precise anastomosis.

The surgeon places each suture bite under direct visualization to ensure precision and prevent gaps that could lead to postoperative leakage. The posterior aspect of the anastomosis is performed in a running fashion, while the bedside assistant uses suction to clear leaking bile for a better view.

The surgeon uses a suture starting from the nine o'clock position to create the anterior aspect of the anastomosis, forming the front wall. The back wall of the anastomosis has already been completed.

The surgeon uses a regular needle holder to pass each needle precisely during the robotic-assisted anastomosis. As the procedure nears completion, an anchoring stitch is carefully placed at the hilum to reduce tension and prevent bile leakage. Another anchoring stitch is placed at the three o'clock position to suspend the Roux limb in two places, ensuring no injury to the liver's inflow structures.

The surgeon uses the robotic system to place a drain through one of the port sites in the right quadrant. The surgical field is clear, and the operation is completed with minimal estimated blood loss of fifty cc.
